note_data.title: Motion and Force
keyPoints: Motion is the change in position of a body with respect to time and place
Force is a push or pull that changes or tends to change the state of rest or uniform motion of a body
Newton's First Law: A body at rest remains at rest and a body in motion remains in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force
Newton's Second Law: The rate of change of momentum is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction of force
Newton's Third Law: To every action there is always an equal and opposite reaction
Momentum is the product of mass and velocity of a moving body (p = mv)
Inertia is the property of matter by virtue of which it resists any change in its state of rest or uniform motion
Weight is the force with which earth attracts a body towards its center (W = mg)
Friction always opposes the relative motion between two surfaces in contact

definitions.definition: Force is a push or pull that changes or tends to change the state of rest or uniform motion of a body.
Momentum is the product of mass and velocity of a moving body.
Inertia is the property of matter by virtue of which it resists any change in its state of rest or uniform motion.
Weight is the force with which earth attracts a body towards its center.
Friction is the force that opposes the relative motion between two surfaces in contact.

importantQuestions.question: State Newton's First Law of Motion.
importantQuestions.answer: A body at rest remains at rest and a body in motion remains in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force. This law is also called the law of inertia.

importantQuestions.question: Derive the equation F = ma from Newton's Second Law of Motion.
importantQuestions.answer: According to Newton's Second Law: F ∝ (change in momentum)/time. F ∝ (mv - mu)/t. F ∝ m(v-u)/t. F ∝ ma (since a = (v-u)/t). Removing proportionality: F = kma. In SI units, k = 1. Therefore, F = ma. This is the mathematical form of Newton's Second Law.

Newton's First Law is also known as:
If a car moving at 20 m/s has mass 1000 kg, its momentum is:

commonMistakes: Students confuse mass and weight - remember weight = mg, not just mass
Writing momentum formula as p = m/v instead of p = mv
Forgetting to state Newton's laws word-for-word as given in textbook
Not drawing proper free body diagrams when solving force problems
Mixing up action-reaction pairs in Newton's Third Law examples
examTips: This chapter usually has 2-3 MCQs and 1 long derivation question in every BSEK paper
Always memorize Newton's three laws exactly as written in STBB textbook
Practice drawing free body diagrams - they appear frequently in numerical problems
Learn examples of all three laws with Pakistani context (rickshaw, truck on GT Road, etc.)
note_data.keyDiagram: Free Body Diagram showing all forces acting on a body - draw the object as a dot with arrows representing forces (weight downward, normal force upward, friction opposite to motion direction, applied force in direction of motion). Label each force clearly.
